children.text: So what would you all do there?

He did a lot of social work. We started with him under his auspices, we started the Young Careers Club based out of La Joya Middle School, which was up the street. It is the junior high that feeds into Lanier, and now it's a magnet school believe it or not. But, at that time we organized the first careers day at the junior high level, I think in the state of Texas. Now, I might be wrong, but you can ask him that. We had over 400 students which at that time was phenomenal. We had all these people, professionals coming in to talk to these junior high kids about going to school, staying in school, and the different jobs that were available. Professional jobs- we weren't talking about carpenters, plumbers, and all that- although they're honorable professions also. We wanted to get them interested in higher ed.

I remember reading through the hearings for the Civil Rights Commission and one of the complaints was that the students didn't have sort-of, mentors coming in to talk about professional jobs. I remember one students said all he remembered seeing was someone from, I think it was someone from the jail, or something that came in. (Laughter) "Don't go to jail" (laughter) This must have been this Young Careers Club must have been something they looked forward to-

That was in April, I don’t remember the exact date, but it was in April of 1970. That I do remember the year. It was the first careers junior high school- you know, ever, ever. And it was a tremendous success. His thing was, “Yeah, you need to stay and do higher ed.” Mostly.

And then we would do research, you know we'd do research on several different projects you know, mostly in the legislative area. I can't tell you too much about that because I don’t really- other than that, and some of the case work that we did. You know with the elderly and the poor people, the students. And I was a student myself at that time, so yeah.
Can you tell me a little about what the Guadalupe Center is and sort of what it provides to the community?

tags.tag: Question About Places of San Antonio

children.text: It provides support, number one. At that time, it had a lot of after school activities for the kids- basketball, you know. After school activities, a place to come hang out. Did a lot of social work. A lot of community meetings there. Everything a community center does, we had it there. And that was just all around there. They had the Enman, they had the House of Neighborly, The Guadalupe, they had the Good Samaritan, and Wesley. All the way to the south side they had the Wesley Community Center. They had all of the community centers around there that provided activities for these kids. Basically, it was like, it kind of kept them out of the streets. You know, that's what that was. You don't have that- well you do have it now but it is becoming more and more privately funded. But at that time there was a lot of money set aside for- (coughing). So, I was with Joe Bernal, I would say from 1969 to 70-71. I was with him for a while. So I was with him at the Guadalupe and I was with him at the Commission for Mexican American Affairs.
At this time, are you also in the Young Democrats?
No, uh well yeah I was in the Young Democrats at that time-for a very short while.

Well, no I did like them. But, actually I got to go see Hubert Humphrey. Who ran with Hubert Humphrey? Was that Mon- Mon- "Mondale?" Was it? Was it Mondale? Anyway, my very first like- I think I had always been exposed to racism once I learned about it when I grew up. Although, I kinda think subconsciously I already knew. We went on a bus- the Young Democrats- we took three busses I believe to Dallas. You are talking about the 1960s, now, to go listen to Hubert Humphrey. And, we stopped in Schulenburg, Texas. I will never forget that, because they did not serve blacks. And they said they had to go to the back. You are like, this was after the civil rights. You are talking “Hey, wait a minute!” So, I remember- I think Joe was with us, Joe Bernal was with us- and he said, “You know what? Let’s all go to the back.” I know they got very pissed off. Those people were just livid.

Hubert Humphrey was the Vice President of the United States from 1965 to 1969, serving under President Lyndon B. Johnson. In 1968, the Democratic Party was divided over the Vietnam war and nominated him for the presidental election with running mate Edmund Muskie (not Walter F. Mondale, who served as VP under Jimmy Carter from 1977 to 1981).
